reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Church and foundation maintainers' documented interest in institutional autonomy and plural provision (Hungarian Constitutional framework recognising maintainer plurality in education law); the value that public goods are legitimately served by non-state actors accountable through their founding mission, not only state administrative control.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; the structural plurality of Hungarian education provision.
reactions.public_good_frame: Equitable access to quality education is served by maintaining diverse, mission-driven providers accountable to their communities and public law, not by collapsing provision into uniform state administration. Maintainer autonomy in admission is part of institutional legitimacy.
reactions.argument: S1 conditions admission reform on extending equity-based rules to church/foundation maintainers through 'renegotiated maintainer agreements' or subsidy conditionality. This recognises our autonomy formally, but operationally subordinates it to state-defined equity quotas. The assumption — 'maintainer consent terms unknown' — signals that the design does not yet know whether we will accept uniform admission rules. Our public interest is twofold: (1) we do not oppose equity; we oppose *uniform prescription* of how we achieve it. A Catholic gymnasium may legitimately prioritise formation in faith alongside ability; a Calvinist school may weight character and community commitment. (2) Subsidy conditionality is a soft prohibition — it makes our legal autonomy contingent on compliance with state criteria, eroding institutional independence. We can support S1 IF admission reform includes a genuine renegotiation allowing maintainers to demonstrate equity outcomes *within* our founding mission, not through state-dictated instrument redesign and quota rules. The coachability argument assumes state-designed exams are more fair — but we have pedagogical reasons to value different selection criteria (aptitude for our curriculum, fit with our community, readiness for our teaching model), not only SES-blind standardisation.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would require: (a) genuine renegotiation of maintainer agreements recognising our right to admit according to mission-aligned criteria while meeting documented equity outcomes at the network level (not per-school quotas); (b) removal of subsidy conditionality as the enforcement mechanism — replace with transparent outcome reporting; (c) explicit recognition that uniform instrument design need not apply to church maintainers if we can demonstrate equivalent or superior equity results through our own selection process. Opposition would firm to 'oppose' if the mechanism requires identical admission rules or treats quota non-compliance as grounds for subsidy withdrawal.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented interest in institutional autonomy and the structural plurality of Hungarian education (Nkt. §4 recognising church and foundation maintainers as legitimate providers); the value that the state's role is to regulate outcomes and equity, not to micromanage the internal structure of diverse providers.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation maintainers; the continued existence and differentiation of non-state selective schools.
reactions.public_good_frame: A plural education system serves the public good by offering families genuinely different pedagogical and formational choices, not simulacra of choice within a uniform structure. The state's equity duty is to ensure that low-SES families are not excluded from *any* pathway; it is not to eliminate selective tracks as such.
reactions.argument: S2 phases down early-entry places through a statutory cap applied across state, church and foundation maintainers. The mechanism assumes 'the cap trajectory can be applied across state, church and foundation maintainers, not only state schools' — but this is flagged as an assumption, not a documented agreement. Our autonomy interest is straightforward: a unilateral statutory cap on our enrolment violates the plural-provision logic that legitimates our existence in the first place. If the state believes early selection is harmful, it can phase down state gymnáziums unilaterally; it cannot impose that structural choice on us without renegotiation. The 'Uncertainties' section correctly notes that 'families do not simply migrate to uncapped non-state selective places as state caps bite' — this is precisely the risk. S2 mechanically increases our selectivity (fewer overall early-entry places means higher-SES concentration among those that remain, absent S1 reforms), while reducing our size. We become a smaller, more elite residue. We can support S2 IF it is coupled with S1 admission reform AND if church/foundation maintainers participate in the cap renegotiation with explicit opt-out rights for schools willing to accept equity-based admission rules. Otherwise, S2 is a backdoor way to shrink non-state provision and channel demand to state schools while preserving the state's selective tracks — the asymmetry is the problem.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would require: (a) explicit renegotiation of maintainer agreements, not unilateral statutory imposition; (b) S1-style equity admission reform applied to our schools *if we accept the cap*, or explicit exemption from the cap if we maintain autonomous admission; (c) protection of our advanced-programme funding, not just state-school general-track funding. Opposition would soften to 'no_position' if evidence showed that non-state demand is NOT migrating to our selective places during state cap-down (currently moderate confidence it does migrate). It would firm to 'oppose' if the cap is imposed without renegotiation.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented legal status of church and foundation maintainers as integral to Hungarian education structure (Alaptörvény, Nkt. §4); the organisational interest in preserving the 6-year and 8-year gimnázium tracks as a distinct pedagogical pathway; the value that curricular and formational differentiation at the lower-secondary level serves genuine diversity of need.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers whose identity and competitive advantage depend on early-entry selective tracks (6-year and 8-year gymnáziums). This includes Catholic, Calvinist, Reformed, Jewish and other church-founded secondary schools for which the early track is a core feature of their provision.
reactions.public_good_frame: Elimination of early selection structures the entire lower-secondary system around a single, common pathway. This reduces the ability of non-state maintainers to offer pedagogically coherent, mission-aligned education. While equity is a public good, so is plurality — parents' ability to choose schools aligned with their family's educational philosophy and their child's pace. S3 privileges equity-through-structural-homogeneity over equity-through-access-to-plural-provision. It is a legitimate choice, but not the only one, and it is presented as the only legitimate structural option.
reactions.argument: S3 abolishes the 6/8-year tracks entirely, moving all selection to age 14. This is presented as serving equity by delaying sorting and keeping high-achievers in common tracks to improve peer composition for low-SES pupils. Our opposition rests on four grounds: (1) The core mechanism depends on the asymmetric-peer-effect channel, which the scenario itself marks as 'contested' and 'Hungarian-specific causality undocumented.' The equity case is not strong enough to justify the elimination of an entire structural form. (2) The scenario's own Uncertainties section states: 'Whether sorting does not simply migrate to school choice between general schools and to non-state maintainers — this is a load-bearing assumption that S3's own equity_impact identifies as the central risk; it is asserted, not evidenced.' If middle-class flight to church schools materialises, S3 eliminates our distinctive pathway while failing to achieve its equity goal. We are used as the residual escape valve for a reform that does not work. (3) The implementation explicitly acknowledges that 'if the pact fails, fall back to S2 phase-down rather than an unanchored top-down mandate' — but there is no described pact mechanism. This is not negotiation; it is ultimatum with a fallback. (4) Teacher shortage is documented as strong evidence; S3 requires massive retraining in differentiated instruction. Our schools often have more pedagogical stability than state schools; S3 forces us into a model (comprehensive, heterogeneous lower-secondary) that contradicts our teaching model and our market position. We are not opposed to equity reforms. We are opposed to structural elimination of our pathway without genuine multiparty negotiation and without evidence that the reform will work.
reactions.condition_to_change: Opposition would soften to 'conditional' if: (a) a genuine cross-party, multiparty pact (including maintainer representatives) preceded legislation, with explicit negotiated carve-outs for non-state maintained schools that maintain equity commitments; (b) S3 were implemented initially only in volunteer districts with a decade-long evaluation before any mandatory phase-down, allowing us to see whether equity gains materialise and whether middle-class flight does occur; (c) evidence that the asymmetric-peer-effect channel works in Hungary (not merely extrapolated from Finland/Poland) at the age groups and SES gradients relevant to Hungarian selection; (d) a binding commitment to maintain or increase funding for church-maintained schools under S3, not treat them as the residue. Opposition would firm to 'oppose' (from conditional) if S3 proceeds without renegotiation or pilot evaluation.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Documented interest in preserving the structural autonomy and distinctiveness of church and foundation maintainers; the value that regulation of equity outcomes (not structural mandate) is the appropriate state role; the organisational interest in continuing to operate selective 6/8-year tracks as a core product.
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; the continuation and quality of selective-track provision as a key differentiator and market position.
reactions.public_good_frame: Public equity is served by raising the quality and outcomes of general schools, not by structural elimination of selective pathways. The state's equity duty is to ensure access based on ability and to compensate disadvantage, not to prevent families from choosing selective education. Targeted funding, rigorous standards and network clustering preserve both equity goals and institutional plurality.
reactions.argument: S4 retains the 6/8-year structure and compensates by raising general-school quality through targeted funding, curriculum standards and school clustering (the Portuguese model). This serves our interests directly — we continue operating selective gymnáziums — but it also serves equity, because it does not assume that selective tracks are inherently inequitable. They are only inequitable if good alternatives do not exist. S4 creates good alternatives. The mechanism is politically sustainable (no structural reversal risk like S2/S3 face), educationally coherent (it does not require massive teacher retraining in differentiation, which S3 assumes is possible but evidence marks as low-confidence), and it respects institutional autonomy. We can support quality improvement in general schools without surrendering our own selective mission. The cost risk flagged — 'Budget vulnerability: compensation lines are the first cut in fiscal consolidation' — is real and shared by us; we have the same interest as the state in protecting multi-year funding commitments. Our support is conditional on two things: (1) S4 includes explicit protection of non-state selective provision, not just state general-school compensation, so that the plural structure is not inadvertently eroded by funding skew toward general tracks; (2) we are not required to participate in clustering arrangements that blur our institutional identity or impose state curriculum alignment on our core mission subjects (religion, philosophy, character formation). The 'Uncertainties' section correctly notes that no country has demonstrated that compensation can offset early-sorting effects at Hungary's age-10/12 selection — this is empirically honest, but it does not argue against trying S4. It argues for treating S4 as a time-limited pilot, with evaluation and possible escalation to S2/S3 if outcomes do not improve. We can live with that bargain.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support would weaken to 'conditional' if: (a) S4 included implicit defunding of selective tracks through budget reallocation solely to general schools, without a protected-funding floor for non-state selective provision; (b) participation in school clustering were mandatory and required standardisation of curriculum or mission content in core subjects; (c) targeted-funding formulas were designed in ways that penalised church schools serving mixed-SES populations (e.g., rural Catholic schools serving both advantaged and disadvantaged families). Support would firm to 'conditional' only if the budget vulnerability becomes acute — if successive governments cut the compensation lines faster than they are built. It would shift to 'oppose' only if S4 were used as a deliberate stalking horse for later structural phase-down (S2/S3), which the implementers conceal in advance.
